title.zh: 激光刻字玻璃：1萬年不壞的數位存儲革命
title.en: Glass as the Digital Time Capsule: How Laser Writing Could Store Data for 10,000 Years

summary.zh:
【總結】這項研究揭開了數位時代的存儲革命：科學家開發出「Silica」系統，透過飛秒雷射在玻璃中刻寫微型3D像素（voxel），成功實現每立方毫米0.678G位元的超密集儲存，且資料壽命超過1萬年。這不僅解決了數位資料快速腐壞的危機，更以90%的能源效率超越傳統儲存技術，為雲端資料庫提供永續存續的解決方案。

【研究問題】人類資料量每3年翻倍，但現有數位儲存媒體（如硬碟）平均壽命僅3-5年，需頻繁遷移造成巨大成本。過去研究試圖用玻璃儲存，卻受限於寫入速度慢、能量消耗高，且無法同時滿足容量、穩定性與成本要求。Silica突破性地解決了這些關鍵瓶頸，讓玻璃真正成為可靠的「數位時間膠囊」。

【研究方法】研究團隊設計兩種創新型寫入方式：①「類單脈衝」技術讓玻璃形成「雙軸異向性微孔」，以偏振光調節儲存符號；②「單脈衝相位寫入」在硼矽酸鹽玻璃中製造折射率變化，避免散射干擾。他們更結合機器學習解碼，用類神經網路從光學影像中還原資料，並以閉環控制系統動態調整雷射能量，確保每顆微像素寫入精度。

【結果】實驗顯示，Silica在單張120mm玻璃板上儲存2.02TB資料（相當於5000部電影），寫入速度達65.9Mbit/s（4倍於單光束）。加速老化測試預測：室溫下資料可保存10,000年以上。更驚人的是，系統自動化程度高，每批測試寫入數十億個微像素仍能保持0.0023的品質變異率，證明其商業化可行性。這將徹底改變雲端儲存的未來。
summary.en:
**Summary:** Scientists have engineered a breakthrough data storage system called Silica, using femtosecond lasers to etch microscopic 3D pixels (voxels) into glass. This technology achieves unprecedented storage density of 0.678 Gbit/mm³ while guaranteeing data durability for over 10,000 years at room temperature. Unlike conventional storage media, Silica combines extreme energy efficiency (8.85 nJ/bit) with high-speed multi-beam writing, offering a viable solution to the global data preservation crisis. By treating glass as a digital time capsule, this system could redefine archival storage for the next millennium.

**Problem:** With digital data doubling every three years, current storage media degrade within years, requiring costly, energy-intensive migration. Previous glass-based storage solutions suffered from slow write speeds, high energy consumption, and unreliable error correction. The critical gap: no system could simultaneously meet industry demands for capacity, longevity, energy efficiency, and scalability. Silica addresses this urgent need for sustainable digital preservation.

**Approach:** The team developed two novel voxel writing regimes: 1) Pseudo-single-pulse birefringent writing creates elongated nanovoids using precisely timed seed and data pulses, while 2) single-pulse phase writing induces controlled refractive index changes in borosilicate glass. Crucially, they implemented closed-loop emission monitoring to stabilize laser energy during writing and employed convolutional neural networks to decode noisy images. This combination of hardware innovation (e.g., 4-beam parallel writing) and machine learning enabled reliable data retrieval from complex 3D structures.

**Results:** Silica achieves 2.02 TB of usable capacity per 120mm glass platter with 90% less energy than traditional methods. Its 65.9 Mbit/s throughput—scalable to 16 beams—exceeds previous glass storage records. Accelerated aging tests using Arrhenius plots confirmed stability exceeding 10,000 years at room temperature. Most impressively, the system maintains a quality factor variation of just 0.0023 across billions of voxels, with error correction enabling lossless data recovery. This breakthrough makes glass-based archival storage commercially viable for the first time.

keyArticle.title: Laser writing in glass for dense, fast and efficient archival data storage
